name: McWay Falls Overlook Trail
highlights: 80-foot waterfall onto a protected cove beach. Wheelchair-accessible.

name: Partington Cove Trail
highlights: Hidden rocky cove through a hand-carved tunnel in the cliffside.

name: Pfeiffer Falls & Valley View Trail
highlights: The definitive redwood hike in Big Sur. 60-foot waterfall, valley viewpoint.

name: Soberanes Point & Whale Peak
highlights: Coastal bluff loop with sweeping Pacific views. Sea otters, seals, migrating whales.

name: Creamery Meadow / Bluffs / Panorama Loop
highlights: Most complete single-day experience in Big Sur. Meadows, bluffs, redwood canyons, remote beach.

name: Tanbark Trail & Tin House Loop
highlights: Hardest and most rewarding hike. 360-degree views from ruins of the Tin House.

name: Buzzard's Roost Trail
highlights: Local favorite for sunset. Ridge viewpoint overlooking the Pacific.

name: Cypress Grove Trail (Point Lobos)
highlights: One of only two remaining native Monterey cypress forests on Earth.

name: Bird Island Trail / China Cove (Point Lobos)
highlights: Most photographed spot in Point Lobos. Deep emerald bowl framed by white cliffs.

name: Kayaking Monterey Bay
location: Cannery Row, Monterey — launches from Breakwater Cove
highlights: Sea otters rest in the kelp beds of Monterey Bay and regularly approach kayaks — the most intimate wildlife encounter in the orbit
Monterey Bay Kayaks is the established operator, launching from Breakwater Cove near the aquarium
Guided tours available for first-timers; rental for experienced paddlers
Water temperature is 50–55°F year-round — layers under a wetsuit are essential
bookingWindow: Book at montereybaykayaks.com — guided tours recommended for first visit

name: Scuba Diving — Point Lobos & Monastery Beach
location: Point Lobos State Natural Reserve and Monastery Beach, Carmel
highlights: Among the richest dive environments in North America — the kelp forests of Point Lobos are exceptional
Nudibranchs, rockfish, lingcod, harbor seals, and sea otters in the same dive
Point Lobos requires advance dive permit (831-624-8413 — only Whalers and Bluefish Coves)
Drysuit strongly recommended — 50°F water year-round
bookingWindow: Point Lobos dive permit: 831-624-8413. Gear rental from Aquarius Dive Shop in Monterey.

name: Highway 1 — Road Cycling
distance: Up to 90 miles full corridor; Carmel → Nepenthe is 47 miles
highlights: One of the great road rides in the world — 90 miles of Pacific Coast Highway with the Santa Lucia Mountains on one side and open ocean on the other
Carmel to Big Sur River Inn (26 miles) is the most manageable day ride; Carmel to Nepenthe (47 miles) for the full epic
Light vehicle traffic outside summer weekends; wide shoulders on most sections; Bixby Creek Bridge at mile 13 is the iconic photo stop
Bike rental from Sports Basement in Monterey or Joselyn's Bicycles in Carmel
bookingWindow: Self-guided — road bike rental from Joselyn's Bicycles (Carmel) or Sports Basement (Monterey)

name: 17-Mile Drive — Cycling
highlights: Cyclists enter free (vehicles pay $12.25) — one of the few advantages cyclists have over drivers
Wind through Del Monte Forest past Ghost Tree, Seal Rock, Bird Rock, and the Lone Cypress
Quieter by bike than by car; you can stop at every pull-out without worrying about traffic
Start from the Pacific Grove gate for a natural Monterey-to-Carmel arc
bookingWindow: Self-guided — cyclists enter free at any gate

name: Molera Horseback Tours
location: Andrew Molera State Park, Big Sur — 22 miles south of Carmel on Highway 1
highlights: Guided horseback rides through Andrew Molera State Park — meadows, river crossings, and coastal bluffs at a horse's pace
The only horseback riding operation on the Big Sur coast. Family-owned, small groups, well-trained horses.
The 1-hour beach ride crosses the Big Sur River and follows the bluff trail to views of the Pacific — no experience needed
Book the 2.5-hour ride for the full Molera loop through redwood groves and back along the coast
bookingWindow: Book at molerahorsebacktours.com — 1-2 days ahead in summer. Seasonal operation.

name: Limekiln State Park Trail
location: Limekiln State Park, Big Sur — 52 miles south of Carmel on Highway 1
distance: 1 mile round trip (to limekilns); 0.5 miles to waterfall
highlights: Short trail through old-growth redwoods to four 1880s-era limekilns — massive stone furnaces slowly being reclaimed by the forest
A separate spur trail leads to Limekiln Falls, a 100-foot waterfall flowing over limestone-crusted rock face
Far less crowded than McWay or Pfeiffer Falls — this is southern Big Sur, past the tourist density
The limekilns are a rare intersection of industrial history and deep forest. Moss, ferns, and redwood roots slowly pulling the stonework apart.
bookingWindow: Self-guided — $10 day-use parking fee. Limited spots — arrive before 10am on weekends.

name: Whale Watching — Monterey Bay
location: Monterey Harbor, Fisherman's Wharf — boats depart daily
highlights: Monterey Bay is a submarine canyon as deep as the Grand Canyon — the nutrient upwelling draws gray whales (Dec-Apr), humpbacks (Apr-Nov), and blue whales (Jul-Oct)
Year-round cetacean sightings: orcas, dolphins, and porpoises fill the gaps between whale seasons. Marine biologist narration on most boats.
Monterey Bay Whale Watch and Princess Monterey are the established operators. Smaller boats get closer; larger boats are more stable.
Dress in layers. It's 15-20°F colder on the water than on shore. Take seasickness precautions if prone — the canyon creates chop.
bookingWindow: Book 1-2 days ahead at montereybaywhalewatching.com. Blue whale season (Jul-Oct) fills fastest.
